1.pericope_label: A Cry of Desolation and Praise
1.synthesis: Psalm 22 is the most extensively quoted psalm in the passion narratives and one of the most profound laments in the Psalter. It opens with the cry of desolation—“My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? Why are you so far from saving me, from the words of my groaning?” (Ps 22:1)—and traces an arc from absolute abandonment to worldwide praise. The psalmist contrasts YHWH’s past faithfulness to the fathers (22:4–5) with his own present dereliction: “I am a worm and not a man, scorned by mankind and despised by the people” (22:6). Mockers wag their heads and taunt him to trust in God if God truly delights in him (22:8). Bulls and lions surround him (22:12–13); his bones are out of joint, his heart melted like wax (22:14). “They have pierced my hands and feet... they divide my garments among them, and for my clothing they cast lots” (22:16–18). Then the pivot: “For he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one; he has not hidden his face from him but has listened to his cry for help” (22:24). The psalm closes with universal praise: all nations will worship before YHWH; a people yet to be born will proclaim his righteousness (22:27–31).
1.historical_context: Psalm 22 belongs to the individual lament genre and bears the superscription “To the choirmaster: according to The Doe of the Dawn”—likely a musical designation. The specific physical sufferings described—disjointed bones (22:14), parched throat (22:15), pierced hands and feet (22:16), divided garments (22:18)—describe a suffering so extreme that its original historical referent remains unclear. The surrounding enemies as “bulls of Bashan” (22:12) and lions (22:13) are stock images of overwhelming threat. The worldwide scope of the praise-section (22:27–31) exceeds any historical Davidic occasion and anticipates the eschatological gathering of all nations to YHWH.
1.literary_structure: The psalm’s structure is the sharpest oscillation between desolation and praise in the Psalter: complaint (vv. 1–21) and praise (vv. 22–31), with the pivot at v. 24’s declaration that YHWH has not hidden his face. Within the complaint section, the psalmist alternates between address to God and description of enemies, creating a rhythmic movement of appeal and threat. The closing universalism—all nations bowing, a people yet unborn declaring YHWH’s righteousness (22:31)—expands the frame from one sufferer’s deliverance to the cosmic vindication of YHWH before all creation.
1.theological_application: The pivot of Psalm 22 is the declaration that YHWH “has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one” (22:24): the experience of dereliction is not the last word. Communities shaped by Psalm 22 are given permission to voice the full depth of felt divine absence while holding to the framework that YHWH ultimately does not abandon. The progression from individual cry to universal praise models the arc of lament prayer: it is not resolved by the suppression of the complaint but by its being heard. The psalm commends the practice of bringing the full weight of desolation to God, trusting that the one who hears will act.
1.christology: No psalm is more thoroughly woven into the passion narrative than Psalm 22. Christ’s opening cry from the cross quotes it verbatim: “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?” (Matt 27:46). The mockers who wag their heads (22:7) appear at the crucifixion (Matt 27:39). The taunt about trusting in God (22:8) echoes from the crowd and religious leaders (Matt 27:43). The divided garments and cast lots (22:18) are fulfilled literally at the cross (John 19:24). The closing universalism—all nations worshipping, a people yet unborn declaring YHWH’s righteousness (22:27–31)—is the worldwide harvest of the gospel: “All the ends of the earth shall remember and turn to the Lord” through the proclamation of the crucified and risen one.
